old_claim.note: Scribe Schools provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The scoped monuments are inscribed kudurru records; writing is required for the cuneiform charter or dedicatory inscription, while formal scribe schools are not a direct prerequisite.

source_shape.source_locator: Description and curator comments: limestone boundary-stone prepared for sculptures and inscriptions, faces bearing inscription columns, and a charter from Nebuchadnezzar I.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The museum record classifies the object as a boundary-stone/kudurru and describes carved inscription columns plus a charter on the stone.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: A charter text on the monument makes writing part of the artifact itself. The page does not identify formal schooling as the necessary upstream technology.

ontology_before: The graph treated formal scribe schools as the direct literacy prerequisite for kudurru boundary stones, overstating the institutional dependency.
ontology_after: The graph treats writing as the direct prerequisite for inscribed kudurru records while leaving scribal schooling as a broader social context.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-20-kudurru-boundary-stones-scope keeps writing required and scribe_schools absent as a direct prerequisite.
would_reject_if: The node were rescoped to training institutions that produced kudurru scribes rather than the monuments themselves.
A source showed that the first scoped kudurru monuments required formal school institutions rather than only writing and inscription practice.
short_reason: Inscribed kudurru monuments require writing, not formal scribe schools as a direct edge.
